---
title: "FAIR Reproducibility Guidelines"
format:
  revealjs: default
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-depth: 2
    grid:
        sidebar-width: 200px
        body-width: 1500px
    include-in-header: header.html

---

# FAIR Software
## 🌐 Accessibility

### 🌐 Level 1

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Code available on a public forge

The source code can be found on a `git` force host `git`repositories. For instance:

- **[GitLab](https://gitlab.com)** — Open-core; self-host or cloud; good CI/CD; FAIR-aligned transparency.
- **[Codeberg](https://codeberg.org)** — Fully open-source (Gitea-based); privacy-friendly; EU-hosted.
- **[SourceHut](https://sr.ht)** — Minimalist; open protocols; plain-text workflows; fully libre.
- **[Bitbucket](https://bitbucket.org)** — Proprietary; integrates with Atlassian suite; less open.
- **[Gitea](https://gitea.io)** — Lightweight, self-hosted, MIT-licensed; supports FAIR openness.
- **[Forgejo](https://forgejo.org)** — Gitea fork; community-driven; strong open governance.
- **[GRICAD GitLab](https://gricad-gitlab.univ-grenoble-alpes.fr/)** — Academic research-forge (Université Grenoble Alpes + CNRS + INRIA); local GitLab instance for research software. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
- **[IRD Forge](https://forge.ird.fr/)** — Institutional GitLab-based forge by Institut de recherche pour le développement (IRD); supports versioning, CI/CD, public/private code. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
- **[Forgejo / Fordj (France-specific interest)](https://code.gouv.fr/fr/bluehats/autres-forges/)** — Libre community-forge (via DINUM / BlueHats) for French public sector; open-source development. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
- **[GitHub](https://github.com)** — Widely used; strong integration tools; limited openness (proprietary).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Software commit/branch used for publication results

- Within the README file: Doe, J., & Smith, A. (2025). *CoolSim: Climate simulation toolkit* (Version 2.3.1) [Computer software]. GitHub. Commit: 4f8a9d2. https://github.com/exampleuser/coolsim/tree/4f8a9d2
- Within the paper references:
```bib
@software{Doe_CoolSim_2025,
  author       = {Doe, John and Smith, Alice},
  title        = {CoolSim: Climate simulation toolkit},
  year         = {2025},
  version      = {2.3.1},
  url          = {https://github.com/exampleuser/coolsim/tree/4f8a9d2},
  note         = {Commit 4f8a9d2b6e7c4e1a52d9e9e2f3c17b6a1d3f45ac},
}
```
:::


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Open-source License

The License must be clearly indicated in the root of the repository (**LICENSE/COPYING**).
Exemple of valid licenses:

- **[MIT License](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)** — Permissive; free reuse, modify, distribute; attribution only.
- **[Apache 2.0](https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)** — Permissive; attribution + patent grant; high interoperability.
- **[BSD 3-Clause](https://opensource.org/licenses/BSD-3-Clause)** — Permissive; attribution + non-endorsement.
- **[GPL-3.0](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0.en.html)** — Copyleft; derivatives must remain open.
- **[LGPL-3.0](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/lgpl-3.0.en.html)** — Copyleft; Lesser GPL permits use of the library in proprietary programs.
- **[MPL-2.0](https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/MPL/2.0/)** — Weak copyleft; file-level openness; flexible reuse.
- **[CC-BY-4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)** — Attribution; for data/docs; FAIR-compliant openness.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Authors and contributors provided

The authors and contributors should be given with one or several of the following:

- **`AUTHORS` file** — Plain text or Markdown file listing main authors and maintainers.
  Example: `AUTHORS`, `AUTHORS.md`

- **`CONTRIBUTORS` file** — Lists additional contributors or institutional credits.
  Example: `CONTRIBUTORS`, `CONTRIBUTORS.md`

- Within **`README` file** — Optional “Authors” or “Credits” section; human-readable summary.

- Within **Version control management** — With clean commit history, the exact contributor list is metadata.

:::


### 🌐🌐 Level 2

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Code archived on Zenodo

- **Code archived on Zenodo** - Repository release exported to Zenodo for citation and FAIR compliance. Long-term preservation and DOI assignment.
- Can be done via [GitHub–Zenodo integration](https://guides.github.com/activities/citable-code/).

:::

### 🌐🌐🌐 Level 3

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Software Heritage Identifier (SWHID)

- **Code archived on Software Heritage** — Ensures permanent, tamper-proof preservation of source code. Every commit is archived and globally accessible.
- Very easy and reliable.
- Provide a SWHID permanent id.
- Can identify only parts of a code
- See [the "Save code now" procedure](https://archive.softwareheritage.org/save/) to proceed from different origins (git, tar, svn, ...)
<!--
- See [Software Heritage: Archiving GitHub repositories](https://docs.softwareheritage.org/devel/swh-model/platforms.html#github) for instructions on submitting repositories.
-->
:::


### 🌐🌐🌐🌐 Level 4

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# HAL deposit

- **Code deposited in HAL** — Upload your software as a research output to [HAL](https://doc.hal.science/en/deposit_new/).
- Provides an open-access archive with DOI assignment.
- Supports detailed metadata, linking to authors, grants, and publications.
- Long-term preservation with institutional endorsement.
:::


## ↺ Reproducibility

### ↺ Level 1

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Re-runable code
- Description of different files of the code in a README file
- Command to launch the simulation
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Precise description of the workflow
- Identification and description of each file
- Description of the complete workflow including data managment
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Test case(s)
- The repository contains a test case to insure that the software provides correct results
:::

### <span style="color:red"> ↺</span>↺ Level 2

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Workflow manager for automatic execution
Some examples:
- [Nextflow](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/index.html)
- [Snakemake](https://snakemake.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)
- [Luigi](https://luigi.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)
- [openWDL](https://openwdl.org/)
- [CWL](https://www.commonwl.org/user_guide/index.html)
:::

### ↺↺↺ Level 3

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Reproducible software environment
The code comes with some files describing the complete software environement e.g.:
- langage version
- compiler name and version
- interpreter version
- dependances version
This can be achieved through files like Makefiles, manifest.scm, renv (R), venv (Python)
:::

### ↺↺↺↺ Level 4

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Non-regression tests
The code contains various tests to ensure that the results of these tests are the same after modification of the code
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Documentation in CI
The documentation of the code is automatically generated by [Continuous Integration](https://about.gitlab.com/topics/ci-cd/) on a forge.
:::


## <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Re-usable

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 1

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# All functions documented inside the code
- User is able to easily identify the the role of each function
:::

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 2

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Description of each element in a Readme + workflow description for execution
- each file is documented in a Readme file and the user is able to identify the role of each part of the code
and in which order it has to be used
:::

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 3

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# User documentation with examples
- Documentation contains links to theoretical and algorithms aspects
- Also contains a simple test-case description and expected results
:::

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 4

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Developer documentation ("How to contribute?" guide)
- Repository contains a documentation descripbong the rules chosen for the code (variables, ...)
:::


# FAIR Dataset

## 🌐 Accessibility

### 🌐 Level 1

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# data available on an accessible permanent repository

- Zenodo
- other institutional repositories (find examples)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Open-science creative common License

The License must be clearly indicated in the root of the repository (**LICENSE/COPYING**).
Exemple of valid licenses:

- **[CC-BY-4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)** — Attribution; for data/docs; FAIR-compliant openness.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Authors and contributors provided

The authors and contributors should be given with one or several of the following:

- **`AUTHORS` file** — Plain text or Markdown file listing main authors and maintainers.
  Example: `AUTHORS`, `AUTHORS.md`

- **`CONTRIBUTORS` file** — Lists additional contributors or institutional credits.
  Example: `CONTRIBUTORS`, `CONTRIBUTORS.md`

- Within **`README` file** — Optional “Authors” or “Credits” section; human-readable summary.

- Within **Version control management** — With clean commit history, the exact contributor list is metadata.

:::

### 🌐🌐🌐 Level 2

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# RO-crate and metadata extraction

- metadata extracted from file scanning (Solidipes, other examples, ...)
- RO-crate format for storage of metadata
:::

### 🌐🌐 Level 3

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Clean dataset

- README file: dataset description
- Understandable directory structure (not too deep)
- No irrelevent files (empty, temporary)
- Reasonable compression level
- Long-term preservation file formats
:::

## ↺ Reproducibility

### ↺ Level 1

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Reproducible data

It is possible to re-generate the dataset

- With guidance in documentation
- With a script (reproduce.sh, reproduce.py, ...)
:::


### ↺↺ Level 2

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Reproducible software environment

Dependency Specification Methods may be:

- `requirements.txt` — standard pip list of packages. [Docs](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/cli/pip_install/#requirements-file-format)
- `setup.py` — classic Python package metadata + dependencies, used with setuptools. [Docs](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/declarative_config.html)
- `setup.cfg` — declarative alternative to `setup.py` for setuptools. [Docs](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/declarative_config.html)
- `pyproject.toml` — modern build system and dependencies (PEP 518/621), used by Poetry, Flit, etc. [Docs](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/)
- `environment.yml` — Conda environment specification for Python + packages. [Docs](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html#creating-an-environment-yml-file)
- `renv.lock` / renv — environment and lockfile [Docs](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/articles/renv.html)
- `package.json` — main metadata + dependencies [Docs](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v9/configuring-npm/package-json)
- `package-lock.json` — exact versions lockfile [Docs](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v9/configuring-npm/package-lock-json)
- `yarn.lock` — Yarn lockfile [Docs](https://classic.yarnpkg.com/lang/en/docs/yarn-lock/)

:::

### ↺↺↺ Level 3


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Workflow manager for automatic reproduction of data

Usage of a workflow manager for dependency tree representation and management

- General workflow managers
  - **[GNU Make](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/)** — Classic rule-based build automation; tracks dependencies; simple, reproducible task execution.
  - **[Snakemake](https://snakemake.github.io/)** — Rule-based, scalable, reproducible pipelines.
  - **[Nextflow](https://www.nextflow.io/)** — Data-driven workflows, cloud & HPC support.
  - **[Luigi](https://luigi.readthedocs.io/)** — Python task dependency management, batch pipelines.
  - **[Airflow](https://airflow.apache.org/)** — DAG-based orchestration, scheduling, monitoring.
  - **[BlackDynamite](https://gitlab.com/blackdynamite/blackdynamite)** — Automates parametric studies, runs executables with varying parameters, collects and organizes results.


- AI / ML oriented workflow managers
  - **[MLflow](https://mlflow.org/)** — Experiment tracking, model registry, reproducible ML pipelines.
  - **[Kubeflow](https://www.kubeflow.org/)** — Kubernetes-native ML workflows and model deployment.
  - **[Metaflow](https://metaflow.org/)** — Human-centric ML pipelines, versioned and reproducible.
  - **[Weights & Biases (W&B)](https://wandb.ai/)** — Experiment tracking and pipeline monitoring.

- Other / Specialized workflow managers
  - **[Galaxy](https://galaxyproject.org/)** — Accessible bioinformatics workflow platform.
  - **[Nextflow Tower](https://tower.nf/)** — Cloud & cluster monitoring interface for Nextflow.
  - **[Argo Workflows](https://argoproj.github.io/argo-workflows/)** — Kubernetes-native DAG orchestration.
  - **[Prefect](https://www.prefect.io/)** — Modern Python workflow orchestration with retrying and scheduling.

:::



### ↺↺↺↺ Level 4

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Reproducibility test

- Provide routines verifying that re-running workflow reproduces the results
- For costly calculations, may process a subset of the data
- what else ???

:::

## <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Re-usable

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 1

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# criterion 1

text
:::

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 2

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Criterion 2

text
:::

### <i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i><i class="fa fa-recycle"></i> Level 3

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Criterion 3

text
:::


# Resources (summary)

## ACM badges 1/2

One badge by element. Can be attributed independently and cumulated.
*"artifact" = digital object created by the authors to be used as part of the study or generated by the experiment itself.*

| Badge                                                        | Validated elements                                                                                |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Artifacts evaluated: functional (available for the reviewer) | Documented, consistent, complete, exercisable, includes verification/validation                   |
| Artifacts evaluated: re-usable (available for the reviewer)  | Exceeds minimal functionality, carefully documented, well-structured, norms and standards adhered |


## ACM badges 2/2

| Badge                          | Description                                                                          |
|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Artifacts available (publicly) | Author-created artifacts placed on a public repository with DOI or unique identifier |
| Results reproduced             | Main results obtained by another team using artifacts provided by authors            |
| Results replicated             | Main results independently obtained without author-supplied artifacts                |

Source: [Replicability Stamp](https://www.replicabilitystamp.org/). Papers with Replicability Stamp cited 15% more after 1 year, 33% more after 2 years (WoS 2020).


## Benureau / Rougier

| Level | State        | Definition                                                               |
|-------|--------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| R¹    | re-runnable  | Code can be run again as needed                                          |
| R²    | repeatable   | Produces same output over successive runs                                |
| R³    | reproducible | Another researcher can re-obtain same result with original code/data     |
| R⁴    | re-usable    | Easily used and modified by others (comments, documentation, workflow)   |
| R⁵    | replicable   | Article description allows results to be re-obtained without source code |

---

## CODE beyond FAIR 1/2

### OPEN

| Recommendation                       | Level                |
|--------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Publish source code on public forge  | mandatory            |
| Save repository on dedicated archive | mandatory            |
| License code with an open license    | strongly recommended |
| Declare authorship and rightholders  | recommended          |

### DOCUMENT

| Recommendation                            | Level       |
|-------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Choose meaningful names                   | recommended |
| Comment code                              | recommended |
| Provide examples, notebooks, or tutorials | recommended |
| Describe API                              | optional    |

---

## CODE beyond FAIR 2/2

### EXECUTE

| Recommendation                                     | Level       |
|----------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| List software and hardware dependencies            | recommended |
| Provide computational environment                  | optional    |
| Implement a test suite                             | optional    |
| Show real-life usage example with expected results | optional    |

### COLLABORATE

| Recommendation                                 | Level    |
|------------------------------------------------|----------|
| Respond to issues and feature requests         | optional |
| Describe maintenance, features, support limits | optional |
| Explain how to contribute                      | optional |
| Build and animate a community                  | optional |

---

## Other resources

- [Replicability Stamp](https://www.replicabilitystamp.org/)
- [HAL - Le dépôt de logiciels](https://doc.hal.science/ressources/essentiels/CCSD_essentiels_logiciels.fr.pdf)
- [Inria PDF 1](https://inria.hal.science/hal-04697010/file/final-author-version.pdf)
- [Inria PDF 2](https://inria.hal.science/hal-04930405v1/document)
- [Computo Journal Review](https://computo-journal.org/review/)
- [Frontiers in Neuroinformatics Article](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/neuroinformatics/articles/10.3389/fninf.2017.00069/full)
